coredata.dc:title: Sodium vanadates doped boron phosphorus graphene quantum dots: A novel nanosensor for the fluorescence detection of rutin

coredata.dc:description: Rutin, a naturally occurring flavonoid compound, possesses notable antioxidant properties along with anti-inflammatory and antiviral effects. This research aimed to improve the selectivity and high fluorescence behavior of novel nanomaterial BPGQDs@NaV, which was synthesized by hydrothermal methods. Through comprehensive characterization utilizing TEM, SEM, XRD, EDS, FT-IR, UV–Vis, TCS-PC, and XPS techniques, the prepared BPGQDs, NaV, and BPGQDs@NaV were thoroughly examined. The resulting BPGQDs@NaV nanomaterials demonstrated stable, reproducible fluorescence responses and exhibited selective recognition capabilities towards rutin. The sensor developed in this study displayed remarkable performance in rutin detection, offering a broad linear range from 5 to 110 nM and an outstanding detection limit of 15.16 nM. A computational study was used to examine energy, stability, band gap, and how rutin interacted with the BPGQDs@NaV, and it also favored the detection mechanism. A portable smartphone-based sensor was also developed for the detection of rutin.

head.ce:title: Sodium vanadates doped boron phosphorus graphene quantum dots: A novel nanosensor for the fluorescence detection of rutin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Dr BR Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rutin, a naturally occurring flavonoid compound, possesses notable antioxidant properties along with anti-inflammatory and antiviral effects. This research aimed to improve the selectivity and high fluorescence behavior of novel nanomaterial BPGQDs@NaV, which was synthesized by hydrothermal methods. Through comprehensive characterization utilizing TEM, SEM, XRD, EDS, FT-IR, UV–Vis, TCS-PC, and XPS techniques, the prepared BPGQDs, NaV, and BPGQDs@NaV were thoroughly examined. The resulting BPGQDs@NaV nanomaterials demonstrated stable, reproducible fluorescence responses and exhibited selective recognition capabilities towards rutin. The sensor developed in this study displayed remarkable performance in rutin detection, offering a broad linear range from 5 to 110 nM and an outstanding detection limit of 15.16 nM. A computational study was used to examine energy, stability, band gap, and how rutin interacted with the BPGQDs@NaV, and it also favored the detection mechanism. A portable smartphone-based sensor was also developed for the detection of rutin.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Flavonoids, which are polyphenol chemicals, are excellent antioxidants and have protective properties for DNA (de Martino et al., 2012; Papadopoulou et al., 2005). These substances also have physiological effects, including antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, anti-cancer, anti-adipogenic, anti-hemorrhagic, and anti-aging properties (Guardia et al., 2001; Ündeger et al., 2004). Rutin, also known as 3′, 4′, 5, 7-tetrahydroxyflavone-3β-D-rutinoside, has a variety of physiological effects, including lowering blood pressure, diluting blood, and decreasing capillary permeability (Yang et al., 2015). An overabundance of rutin can cause a number of health risks, such as headaches, rashes, impaired vision, excessive CNS stimulation, and heart rate changes (Ganeshpurkar & Saluja, 2017). As a result, developing an affordable, straightforward, accurate, and selective analytical/bioanalytical method is required for its quantification (Mahmoud et al., 2023). The extraction and applications of flavonoids are of great interest to many research and industrial sectors these days, mainly because of their potential health benefits, which include antioxidation, anti-inflammation, anti-heart disease, inhibition of cancer cells, and promotion of skin health and immune system function (Negahdari et al., 2021). The poor selectivity brought on by numerous interfering compounds in complex matrices, which include fruits like apricot (Karaçelik, 2023), grape (G.-Y. Tang et al., 2018), and kiwi (Ma et al., 2019), as well as vegetables like tomatoes (J. Tang et al., 2023) and onion family (Gao et al., 2021), makes it difficult to detect rutin (El Hani et al., 2024).

Currently, the methods used to determine the content of rutin flavonoids primarily rely on large-scale analytical instruments, including capillary electrophoresis (Al-Taweel et al., 2015; Memon et al., 2019), electrochemical method (Kong et al., 2021; Peng et al., 2019), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Kurkin & Kupriyanova, 2020) and high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) (J. Li et al., 2018). The limitations of costly analytical instruments, a laborious testing procedure, and a lengthy testing period limit the application of these methods to online real-time detection and even rapid screening. Despite their efficiency and accuracy, these methods can detect nearly all food ingredients. Fluorescence sensing, which has been a research hotspot and used in a variety of detection disciplines, has the qualities of high sensitivity, quick speed, and ease of use (Chen, Min, et al., 2018; Chen, Xu, et al., 2018; Yan et al., 2018). As an alternative, sensitive and inexpensive analytical methods, such as spectroscopic (spectrophotometric and fluorometric) and electrochemical techniques, were developed to assess rutin, overcoming the challenges of conventional analytical and bioanalytical methods. The electrochemical and fluorometric probes are widely employed for the detection of numerous analytes in various matrices because of their sensitivity and dependability.

Currently, metal nanoclusters, carbon dots (CDs), organic fluorescent dyes, and other nanoscale materials provide the majority of the fluorescence signal used in the study (Ji et al., 2020; Sasikumar & Ilanchelian, 2021; Sharma et al., 2018). These materials may also be used in the creation of integrated and compact fluorescence sensing and detecting systems. The CDs can be mixed with other materials to create nanocomposites, which have been extensively employed in the investigation of fluorescence sensing. They also have outstanding optical properties, good biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity (Duhan et al., 2024b; Pan et al., 2020).

Vanadium oxide compounds, including V2O5, VO2, V2O4, V2O3, and V6O13, have garnered significant attention recently due to their diverse range of intriguing characteristics, which stem from various oxidation states (varying from +2 to +5). Though their characteristics can be easily altered by chemical doping, vanadium oxide-based nanostructures are rarely investigated for this purpose. To tune electrical characteristics conveniently, for example, various compounds can be formed by doping with alkaline or alkaline-earth metal ions. Sodium vanadate has garnered significant interest among these compounds owing to its exceptional mechanical strength, electrical characteristics, and chemical stability (Presura et al., 2003; Yamaura et al., 2002). Furthermore, including sodium ions is anticipated to improve the field emission performance of pure vanadium oxides significantly. These extra sodium ions stabilize the structure when electrons are emitted by positioning themselves to create connections between the layers of vanadium oxide. The sodium vanadium nanomaterial is useful for sensing devices because of its exceptional qualities, which include outstanding chemical and thermal stabilities, a large optical band gap, and redox activity (Nguyen et al., 2015).

Because of their consistent qualities, including chemical stability, low photon energy, and superior catalytic, electrocatalytic, optical, and magnetic capabilities metal vanadate compounds are attractive for use in a range of applications, including solid-state lighting devices, catalysis, and optical lasers. Applications for metal vanadates, specifically copper, nickel, cobalt, zinc, cadmium, and iron vanadates (Gadge et al., 2024; Kalanur et al., 2023; Narsaiah et al., 2024) include gas sensors, supercapacitors, water splitting, photocatalysts, and lithium-ion batteries (Awad et al., 2023; Hasan et al., 2024; Nabgan et al., 2023).

Density functional theory (DFT) is a quantum mechanical modeling method used in physics and chemistry to understand and predict the electronic structure (the distribution of electrons) of atoms, molecules, and solids. It's based on the principles of quantum mechanics and statistical mechanics, focusing on the electronic density rather than the wavefunctions of electrons. DFT helps to predicting molecular structures, bond energies, reaction mechanisms, and spectroscopic properties (Alizadeh et al., 2023; Kumer et al., 2020). Overall, Density Functional Theory has become a widely used and powerful tool in theoretical and computational chemistry, contributing significantly to our understanding of complex systems at the atomic and molecular level (Gholamrezazadeh et al., 2023).

Consequently, because of their useful qualities as sensors, researchers have used graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to identify a wide range of biological compounds, including rutin. Boron phosphorus co-doped graphene quantum dots (BPGQDs) can raise the optical activity of sodium vanadates. In order to measure the selectivity in the presence of various analytes, as well as to monitor the degree of rutin sensitivity in samples, a fluorescence-based sensor formed by a mixture of these two materials was designed in this work based on the properties of BPGQDs and sodium vanadates described herein. Notably, there has been no report on the utilization of Sodium Vanadates/BPGQDs for a fluorometric assay. This work introduces the first-time synthesis of BPGQDs@NaV for highly sensitive fluorescence detection of rutin, complemented by a novel DFT study illustrating the interaction between nanomaterials and rutin. Achieving a remarkably low limit of detection and wide concentration range underscores its potential.

Vanadium pentoxide (V2O5), sodium carbonate, citric acid, boric acid, phosphoric acid, rutin, quercetin, kaempferol, malvidin, isorhamnetin, chlorogenic acid, dopa, cysteine, alanine, glycine, lysine, potassium chloride, magnesium sulphate, sodium hydroxide pellets, zinc sulfate, fructose, riboflavin, Sulphuric acid and concentrated hydrochloric acid were acquired from Loba India. Without additional purification, the analytical grade reagents were all utilized just as supplied. Except as indicated otherwise, all solutions used in this investigation were made from bi-distilled deionized water that had been cleaned using a UV-treated rephile water system.

Sodium Vanadates nanorods were synthesized by using vanadium pentoxides and sodium carbonates. 3 g of vanadium pentoxides and 1 g of sodium carbonate were dissolved in 5 milliliter of ethanol and stir up to 30 min on a magnetic stirrer at 40 °C then transferred the obtained solutions into a crucible and put into a muffle furnace for 6 h at 450 °C. The Sodium Vanadates nanorods were synthesized successfully, and the schematic representation is shown in Scheme 1 .

We have already synthesized BPGQDs in our previously published articles (Duhan et al., 2024a). Dissolving 5 g of citric acid in 10 ml of distilled water, after that the mixture was combined with 2 g of boric acid and 3 ml of phosphoric acid. The combined solution was then heated to 40 °C for 20 min using a magnetic stirrer. Additionally, pour the aforementioned solution into a crucible and heat it to 180 °C for six hours in a muffle furnace. The brown liquid solution was extracted once the mixture cooled to room temperature. The resultant solution was filtered, centrifuged at 12,000 rpm, and then poured into a dialysis bag for 48 h to get rid of large particles. Finally, the produced BPGQDs were placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C for additional characterization and uses.

BPGQDs (3 mL) were mixed with 10 mL DDW. Then, add sodium Vanadates (250, 500 mg) to the BPGQDs solutions and sonicate them for 20 min. The resultant solution was put in a muffle furnace at 185 °C for six hours. The product was extracted using a chemical extraction method, where the initial step involved grinding it thoroughly to enhance surface area for improved extraction efficiency. The resulting material was then dissolved in double-deionized water (DDW) and subsequently filtered to eliminate any unreacted components. Afterward, the solutions were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min. The resulting products obtained were bluish and light green in color, intended for doping with 250 mg and 500 mg of sodium Vanadates, respectively.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were employed to investigate the electronic structure and properties of nanomaterials. In order to explore the interaction between the probe and rutin, the geometries of BPGQDs, BPGQDs@NaV, and BPGQDs@NaV/rutin were optimized through density functional theory (DFT) calculations conducted in Gaussian16 with the B3LYP hybrid functional. Initially, a geometry optimization was performed utilizing the 6-31G(d) basis set. Subsequently, energy calculations and HOMO, LUMO structures were carried out using the larger 6-311G(d,p) basis set. The HOMO/LUMO gap of each species was determined as the minimum energy difference between the HOMO and LUMO levels at a specific point, and Frontier molecular orbital analysis (HOMO-LUMO) was conducted to elucidate the quenching mechanism.

The produced sodium Vanadates/BPGQDs fluorescence response to rutin and its analogs was evaluated using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Dissolved 50 μL of sodium vanadates/BPGQDs in 3 ml of distilled water and then added varying concentrations of rutin (5–110 nM) for fluorometric measurement. The mixture was then allowed to sit at room temperature for five minutes. After excitation at 330 nm, the solution was finally measured at λem of 420 nm. The fluorescence decay curve was recorded after excitation with 330 nm light.

To fabricate the BPGQDs@NaV sensing probe for detecting rutin, BPGQDs are initially synthesized using the hydrothermal method. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image depicts spherical BPGQDs nanoparticles with uniform distribution (Fig. 1a). SEM images of BPGQDs also support the HR-TEM image, and the particles are spherical in shape (Fig. 1b). The particle size distribution of BPGQDs, illustrated in Fig. 1c ranges from 2 to 8 nm, with an average lateral size of 3 nm. This narrow and uniform particle size distribution, coupled with excellent dispersion, is expected to augment the sensing capabilities of the probe significantly.

The morphologies of NaV and BPGQDs@NaV were examined using FE-SEM. Fig. 1d illustrates the characteristic rods-like structure of NaV deposited on glass. The modification of the BPGQDs with NaV nanorods was assessed using HR-TEM and FE-SEM. As depicted in Fig. 1e and Fig. 1f, the BPGQDs@NaV nanomaterials form small spherical cluster structures. A structured inverse opal arrangement with three-dimensional porosity emerged also shown in SEM images. This structure is anticipated to contribute to the precise identification of rutin molecules significantly (Xie et al., 2022). EDS elemental analysis of developed BPGQDs@NaV is shown in Fig.S1, which helps to confirm the successful synthesis of BPGQDs@NaV. Here, we found that boron, carbon, oxygen, phosphorus, sodium, and vanadium were present on the surface of nanomaterials. Fig. 2 (a-f) shows the EDS mapping of sodium, vanadium, phosphorus, oxygen, carbon, and boron. Fig. 2g shows the EDS mapping of all elements. All these aforementioned data prove the successful fabrications of BPGQDs@NaV nanomaterials. The average particle size of BPGQDs@NaV was approximately 8 nm shown in Fig. 2h.

Fig. 3a displays the XPS full survey spectra of the as-prepared BPGQDs@NaV nanomaterials. The characteristic peaks of C1s (285.08 eV), V2p (517.02 and 530.2 eV), and O1s (532.04 eV), B1s (191.16 eV), P2p (134.1 eV) and Na1s (1071 eV) are clearly discernible. These findings suggest the successful attachment of BPGQDs onto the NaV nanorods. The high-resolution spectra of B1s are shown in Fig. 3b. Here, we found a peat at 191.58 eV that was due to BO. Furthermore, the peak deconvolution of V2p reveals the binding energies of 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 at 517.2 eV and 524.6 eV, respectively, indicative of the characteristic peak of V5+ (Patel et al., 2024) in NaV, as shown in Fig. 3c. The deconvoluted C1s spectra of BPGQDs@NaV reveal a broad peak at 284.9 eV, corresponding to the CC functional groups of BPGQDs. Additionally, two smaller peaks at 286.4 and 289.01 eV are identified as CN and CO, (Duhan & Obrai, 2023a) respectively as shown in Fig. 3d. Furthermore, the deconvolution of the P2p spectrum (Fig. 3e) indicates the presence of C-O-P (133.48 eV) and C-PO3 (135.64 eV). Moreover, the deconvoluted peak of O1s at 531.4 eV corresponds to the OV functional group of V2O5, while 532.9 eV is attributed to the abundant hydroxyl groups (OH) and 536.12 eV was due to OH of BPGQDs@NaV (Fig. 3f).

Two different conc. of NaV doped into BPGQDs 250 mg and 500 mg. XRD spectra reveals the amorphous nature of BPGQDs@NaV nanomaterials and a broad peak was observed at 23.80 and 27.10 in the case of BPGQD+(500 mg) NaV and BPGQD+(250 mg) NaV doped as shown in Fig. 4a. In the FT-IR spectra of NaV the peak observed at 842 cm−1 corresponds to the symmetric stretching vibration of V–Ob bonds coordinated at the bridge, while those detected at 938 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibration of terminal VO bonds. Absorption bands occurring near 1450 cm−1 are attributed to the vibration of NaO bonds (Bi et al., 2021) shown in Fig.S2. The FT-IR spectrum of BPGQD showed peaks at 1006, 1636, and 3300 cm−1 due to the PO, CO, and OH bands, respectively. In the spectrum of BPGQD@NaV, in addition to the distinctive peaks of NaV and BPGQDs, the stretching vibration of OH bonds emerges around 3300 cm−1, CO bands appear at 1636 cm−1, NaO bands are observed at 1451 cm−1, and peaks at 1058 and 950 cm−1 signify VO and V-Ob bands, indicating the formation of BPGQDs@NaV as shown in Fig. 4b. In the FT-IR spectrum of BPGQDs@NaV, when we examined the sample containing 250 mg of doped NaV, we observed no distinct peaks within the 900–1100 cm−1 range and no discernible NaO peaks around 1400 cm−1 (Wang et al., 2020). However, upon increasing the NaV doping to 500 mg, sharp peaks emerged. Consequently, we opted for the 500 mg doping amount, as illustrated in Fig. 4c. Also, 500 mg doped NaV in BPGQDs has the highest PL intensity than 250 mg doped shown in Fig. 5a.

UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of the BPGQDs@NaV were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively, to examine the photosensitive properties of the nanomaterials. Under the light of a UV lamp, the BPGQDs@NaV exhibit vivid photoluminescence. The synthesized BPGQDs@NaV UV–Vis absorption spectrum exhibits a peak at approximately 250 nm. These are attributed to the aromatic C––C bonds π → π* transition of the CO and VO bonds, as shown in Fig. 5b. For pure sodium vanadates, the UV–Vis peaks appear at nearly 215 nm by modification with BPGQDs, the shift, in peak appears towards redshift. These transitions are associated with the BPGQDs@NaV charges on surface states and their fluorescence behavior.

Fig. 5c displays the emission spectra of BPGQDs@NaV at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 290 to 380 nm. The fluorescence intensity emitted at 420 nm demonstrates an increase from 290 to 330 nm excitation, followed by a decrease within the 340 to 380 nm range. These findings indicate that the emission wavelength of BPGQDs@NaV remains independent on the excitation wavelength, with the maximum peak emission occurring at 330 nm. The fluorescence property, attributed to the presence of carboxylic functional groups and heteroatom doping on the surface of GQDs, suggests their potential for superior analyte sensing capabilities.

The indispensability of good stability and exceptional properties for broadening the utility of BPGQDs@NaV was investigated under various conditions such as UV lamp radiation, high saline solution, pH variations, and extended periods. The stability of the BPGQDs@NaV was assessed under UV lamp radiation, revealing negligible change in fluorescence intensity of BPGQDs@NaV even after 60 min of exposure (Fig.S3d), indicating superior resistance to photobleaching. In sensing applications, maintaining consistent fluorescence intensity is crucial for achieving reliable and reproducible measurements. The stability of fluorescence intensity in BPGQDs@NaV under high ionic strength settings makes them suitable for environmental monitoring tasks where accurate and reliable detection of pollutants or analytes is required. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of BPGQDs@NaV remained consistent across different NaCl concentrations, demonstrating remarkable stability even at high ionic strength settings (4.0 M, Fig.S3b), which is advantageous for diverse environmental and sensing applications suggests they are robust against salt-induced quenching or other adverse effects. Additionally, fluorescence stability in various pH solutions was examined, with results depicted in Fig.S3c. Initially, fluorescence intensity was low in the acidic range, This suggests that under acidic conditions, either the nanomaterials are less emissive or there may be quenching effects due to protonation or other interactions that reduce fluorescence emission. As the pH of the solution increases from acidic to basic conditions, the fluorescence intensity of BPGQDs@NaV steadily rises. The fluorescence intensity peaks around pH 9–10. This pH range is often optimal for maintaining stable fluorescence emission in many types of quantum dots and fluorescent probes. Beyond pH 10, the fluorescence intensity starts to decline. This decline can be attributed to various factors such as aggregation of nanoparticles, degradation of the surface passivation layer, or other chemical changes that affect the quantum dot's ability to emit light. These findings collectively highlight the immense potential of BPGQDs@NaV across a spectrum of industries and in biosensing and environmental monitoring applications. Notably, after a six-month period, the fluorescence intensity of BPGQDs@NaV remained constant (Fig.S3a), affirming their enduring stability over time. This stability is crucial for applications requiring reliable and reproducible fluorescence measurements over extended periods, such as long-term environmental monitoring or continuous sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Building the nanosensor based on BPGQDs@NaV
ce:section.ce:para: Exploring the application of BPGQDs@NaV in fluorescence sensors was motivated by their excellent photostability and fluorescence properties. In our investigation, we targeted a range of flavonoids, amino acids, and metal ions, including quercetin, dopa, cysteine, alanine, glycine, lysine, potassium chloride, magnesium sulfate, sodium hydroxide pellets, zinc sulfate, fructose, riboflavin, Sulphuric acid, kaempferol, malvidin. Fig. 5d illustrates that only rutin significantly quenches the fluorescence spectra of BPGQDs@NaV, indicating the material's selectivity towards rutin. The fluorescence intensity of BPGQDs@NaV at 420 nm gradually diminishes with increasing concentrations of rutin (5–110 nM), as depicted in Fig. 5a, affirming the nanosensor's capability for precise and reliable rutin sensing.

Fig. 5e depicts the titration method and the linear correlation between BPGQDs@NaV and rutin with an R2 value of 0.9954, a robust linear relationship is observed between the fluorescence ratio (F0/F) and rutin concentration within the range of 5–110 nM. Moreover, the Limit of Detection (LOD) for rutin detection is determined to be 15.16 nM, significantly surpassing the capabilities of existing methods. Table S1 provides a comparative analysis between our constructed nano-sensor and previously published analytical methods for rutin detection, revealing that our method offers a comparable minimum detection limit and a broader linear range. These results collectively attest to the superior selectivity, strong anti-interference properties, and exceptional sensitivity of the “turn-off” fluorescence nanosensor for precise rutin detection in sample analysis.

We conducted additional experiments to investigate the quenching mechanism between rutin and BPGQDs@NaV. Initially, we assessed the fluorescence stability of the BPGQDs@NaV to eliminate potential quenching factors. We measured the maximum fluorescence emission of BPGQDs@NaV excited at 330 nm. The results showed no significant decrease in fluorescence intensity at room temperature over a period of 180 days, Furthermore, even after five consecutive scans using a luminescent spectrometer, only a negligible decrease of less than 1% in fluorescence was observed. These findings collectively demonstrate the exceptional photostability of the BPGQDs@NaV. As depicted in Fig. 5f, rutin exhibited an absorption band at 252 nm and 350 nm. The maximum excitation wavelength for BPGQDs@NaV was at 330 nm. When excited at this wavelength, BPGQDs@NaV demonstrated a robust fluorescence emission peak at 420 nm. Fig.S10 shows the spectral overlay of excitation spectrum of BPGQDs@NaV, absorption spectra of rutin and emission spectra of BPGQDs@NaV. Exploring the intriguing sensing mechanisms of BPGQDs@NaV, which exhibit remarkable performance in detecting rutin through fluorometric modes, proved to be an enthralling endeavor due to their exceptional efficacy.

In general, potential quenching mechanisms encompass the inner filter effect (IFE), fluorescence resonant energy transfer (FRET), static quenching effect (SQE) (Cheng et al., 2006), and dynamic quenching effect (DQE), among others the FRET quenching mechanism typically necessitates a substantial spectral overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of a quencher and the emission spectrum of a fluorescence agent (Chen, Yu, & Wang, 2018; Shi et al., 2015). However, in the case of BPGQDs@NaV and rutin, the emission wavelength of BPGQDs@NaV overlapped with the absorption spectrum of rutin (see Fig. 5f). The quenching mechanism due to overlapping UV absorption and fluorescence emission is typically explained using two common mechanisms: Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and Inner filter effects (IFE). That further confirm by calculating fluorescence lifetime. In IFE, no significant change in fluorescence lifetime was observed when analytes were added. However, in FRET, a significant change in fluorescence lifetime was observed when analytes were present compared to when they were absent. The fluorescence lifetime of BPGQDs@NaV in the absence and presence of rutin was noted when excited at 330 nm. The fluorescence lifetime of BPGQDs@NaV experienced a significant decrease upon the introduction of rutin, diminishing from 6.07 ns to 5.82 ns (as illustrated in Fig. 6a and Fig. 6b). This observation suggests that fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) induced PL quenching rather than IFE, as indicated by prior research (Sun et al., 2024), where the FL lifetime of the donor remained unchanged in IFE but notably decreased during FRET. This suggests that the quenching mechanism between BPGQDs@NaV and rutin involves FRET. The presence of hydroxyl and V/O groups on the surface of the BPGQDs@NaV promotes hydrogen bonding with rutin, thereby reducing the spatial distance between the BPGQDs@NaV and rutin. This phenomenon supports the occurrence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), as documented in several studies. Experimental findings validate the presence of FRET, evidenced by the decrease in photoluminescence intensity of the BPGQDs@NaV. In the BPGQDs@NaV/rutin system, the fluorescence quenching mechanism primarily involves FRET, where the BPGQDs@NaV functions as the donor and rutin serves as the acceptor. The likely optical mechanism of rutin on BPGQDs@NaV may be due to some other factors. Initially, the aromatic structure of rutin may engage in π–π interactions with BPGQDs@NaV, as well as with the aromatic rings of BPGQDs@NaV. Consequently, rutin is likely to adhere to the surface of BPGQDs@NaV through π–π stacking interactions. Furthermore, the hydrogen bonding interaction between the oxygen sites of BPGQDs@NaV and the hydroxyl (–OH) groups of rutin also contributes positively to rutin detection. In the FT-IR spectrum of BPGQDs@NaV and mixing of rutin in the BPGQDs@NaV we observed that peaks at 948 cm−1 due to bridging V-Ob and peaks due to NaO at 1436 cm−1 disappear and slight shifts in a band at 1068 cm−1 were found that proves the interaction of rutin with BPGQDs@NaV through hydrogen bonding as shown in Fig. 4d. Therefore, the notable enhancement in sensitivity for rutin detection can be attributed to the collective influence of these interactions, particularly the π–π stacking interaction and the hydrogen bonding interaction.

Computational chemistry has risen as a potent method for examining the sensor and its interaction with analytes, and here, in Density Functional Theory (DFT) was selected as the method for the computational analysis (Hashemi et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2024). In this study we investigate the total energy of BPGQDs, BPGQDs@NaV and BPGQDs@NaV/rutin HOMO, LUMO, and stability as shown in Fig.(S7-S9). Initially, for BPGQDs, the energy is calculated to be −106 KeV. Upon doping of NaV, the total energy of BPGQDs@NaV is found.
to be −142 KeV, and for BPGQDs@NaV/rutin overall energy was −152 KeV indicating a decrease, which implies an increase in the stability of the nanomaterials.

Our experimental findings suggest that the quenching mechanism is FRET, hydrogen bonding, and pi-pi stacking between rutin and BPGQDs@NaV. These experimental results are further corroborated by DFT studies conducted using Gaussian16, where the HOMO-LUMO (Adhikari et al., 2017; Chantarasunthon et al., 2023) energies of BPGQDs@NaV and rutin were calculated using the 631G(d,p) basis set. The HOMO-LUMO energies of BPGQDs were found to be −12.77 eV and − 12.36 eV, while those of BPGQDs@NaV were − 4.70 eV and − 4.16 eV, and for BPGQDs@NaV/rutin were − 7.90 eV and − 7.72 eV respectively as shown in table S1. The optimized structure of BPGQDs, BPGQDs@NaV, and BPGQDS@NaV/rutin were shown in Fig.(S4-S6). The energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), and the HOMO-LUMO energy gap for BPGQDs@NaV and BPGQDs@NaV/rutin are depicted in Fig.8b. The energy differences between the HOMO and LUMO in BPGQDs@NaV and BPGQDs@NaV/rutin were determined to be 0.6 eV and 0.18 eV, respectively. The HOMO, LUMO energies of rutin are-4.66 eV and − 4.37 eV respectively. With the assistance of DFT, we can demonstrate the sensing mechanism for rutin using BPGQDs@NaV. Our experimental findings shed light on the FRET mechanism for the quenching phenomenon. Additionally, we observed that the HOMO and LUMO energy gap of of BPGQDs@NaV are higher than those of rutin, indicating possibility of electron transfer from the LUMO of BPGQDs@NaV to the LUMO of rutin. Therefore, the sensing mechanism involves FRET, hydrogen bonding, and pi-pi stacking, as illustrated in Fig. 8a. Therefore, the findings indicate a reduction in the energy gap following the formation of the complex, thereby enhancing the stability of the system (Zhang et al., 2019). Literature suggests that a decrease in the HOMO-LUMO energy gap of a complex compared to the initial species may lead to fluorescence quenching, whereas an increase in this energy gap may result in enhanced fluorescence intensity as shown in Fig. 8b (Sutariya et al., 2019). Accordingly, our results demonstrate a reduction in the energy gap, indicating quenching, which is favorable to the fluorescence spectra.

The selectivity of BPGQDs@NaV primarily depends on the specific recognition of rutin. The selective sensing was evaluated by comparing the fluorescence response of BPGQDs@NaV with various flavonoids, amino acids, and metal salts. To validate the selectivity of the proposed sensing probe, the fluorescence intensity of other flavonoids with similar chemical structures—including quercetin, kaempferol, malvidin, isorhamnetin, chlorogenic acid, dopa, cysteine, alanine, glycine, lysine, potassium chloride, sodium hydroxide pellets, zinc sulphate, and fructose was measured, and the histogram of fluorescence intensity response is shown in Fig. 6c. The selectivity study indicates that there was almost no significant change in the fluorescence intensity of BPGQDs@NaV in the presence of these species (110 nM). However, a sharp decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed with the same amount of rutin (110 nM). This change in intensity may be due to the existence of a FRET mechanism in the case of rutin, whereas no such mechanism was observed for the other co-existing compounds due to differences in their standard UV–Vis absorption spectra. In summary, the developed fluorescent sensing probe displayed acceptable selectivity for rutin and might be proficient in detecting rutin in the presence of interfering substances. Meanwhile, the anti-interference ability of the developed BPGQDs@NaV was investigated by adding equal amounts of structural analogs to the rutin solution, creating a mixed system shown in Fig.S11. The fluorescence response of BPGQDs@NaV in the mixed system was higher than that of each individual, indicating the specific recognition ability of rutin. This selectivity makes BPGQDs@NaV a promising probe for sensitive and specific detection of rutin in various samples. It can be used in biochemical assays, food quality control, and medical diagnostics to detect rutin accurately, even in the presence of other flavonoids. The comparative analysis presented in Table S1 highlights the efficacy of our suggested approach utilizing BPGQDs@NaV materials for rutin detection, outperforming several alternative nanomaterials in the literature. These findings underscore the high effectiveness of our material in rutin detection.

Recent advancements in smartphone technology have revolutionized fluorometric detection and streamlining the process significantly (Duhan & Obrai, 2023b, Y. Li et al., 2023). In this study, the smartphone camera took on the role of a high-performing light detector. It captured images of samples illuminated by 365 nm UV light. Subsequently, the color analyzer software on the phone analyzed the captured images, converting the color information into RGB values. A mobile application developed by XiYi Technology Co., Ltd., called Color Identifier, was utilized for RGB analysis (Karrat et al., 2023). By summing the values of green and blue channels, the concentration of rutin was effectively reflected. A linear correlation between (G + B)/2 values and the concentration of the target substance was established (Fig. 6d), indicating a robust relationship. Results demonstrated a high sensitivity of the sensing platform, exhibiting strong correlation coefficients of 0.9705. This underscores the platform's capability to accurately, conveniently, and sensitively detect rutin and offer a practical solution for quantitative analysis.

In summary, a highly effective novel nanomaterial called BPGQDs@NaV was developed, demonstrating fast response speed and consistent fluorescence for detecting rutin. This study showcased rutin ability to significantly diminish BPGQDs@NaV fluorescence, paving the way for a precise and specific rutin detection method using fluorescent probes. The prepared BPGQDs@NaV were thoroughly examined through comprehensive characterization utilizing XRD, TEM, SEM, EDS, UV–Vis, XPS, FT-IR and TCS-PC, techniques. The TEM and SEM images indicate the formation of spherical cluster-like nanomaterials. EDS and XPS analyses confirm the successful fabrication of the nanomaterials, while TCS-PC provides the lifetime of BPGQDs@NaV both with and without the addition of rutin. This method exhibited excellent sensitivity, accuracy, and stability, making it suitable for swiftly assessing rutin levels in food. The fabricated BPGQDs@NaV displayed vibrant bluish-green fluorescence, remarkable resistance to photobleaching, salt tolerance, and easy water solubility. Leveraging FRET, rutin could selectively quench BPGQDs@NaV fluorescence, enabling the establishment of a highly sensitive detection method. The method's linear range spanned from 5 to 110 nM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 15.16 nM. DFT is also used to find the overall energy and HOMO, LUMO energy gap. Rutin was also detected by using a portable smartphone.
There was no Funding.
There was no ethics approval.

Jyoti Duhan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sangeeta Obrai: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
